question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the key engagements he undertook during his visit to the United States of America over the St Patrick’s Day period.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any assessment that has been made of the impact of the recent sharp increases in fuel costs on businesses and key sectors across Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has given any consideration to how closer co-ordination between Translink and community transport services could deliver better value for money within the public transport system, given the increasing year-on-year loss being reported by Translink on its socially necessary routes.
answer_text: The two sectors have distinct roles: Translink has a statutory role to deliver the scheduled public transport network, including socially necessary routes, while community transport providers deliver targeted, demandresponsive services for users who face difficulty accessing conventional transport.Community Transport services, including DialaLift, already complement the wider network by helping rural residents reach essential services and connect to Translink routes. These providers do not hold the licences, fleet scale or capacity required to operate scheduled public transport routes.  My Department continues to support these services through existing funding programmes, but no formal assessment of further coordination has been carried out.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any (i) scoping; (ii) analysis; and (iii) options appraisal, her Department has undertaken regarding closer co-ordination or integration between Translink and community transport services, from 2023 to present.
answer_text: My Department has not yet undertaken any scoping, analysis or options appraisal on the specific issue of closer co-ordination or integration between Translink and community transport services from 2023 to present.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had with the Community Transport Association and community transport operators, to explore opportunities for closer collaboration with Translink, from 2023 to present.
answer_text: My officials engage regularly with the Community Transport Association and community transport operators on a range of issues.  However, exploring opportunities for closer collaboration with Translink has not been the direct focus of this engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has made of the potential for greater collaboration between Translink and community transport providers, to improve accessibility for passengers who are most removed from conventional public transport services.
answer_text: My Department recognises the distinct and crucial roles of Translink and Community Transport operators in supporting accessible travel.  Community Transport providers deliver the Dial-a-Lift scheme, which supports rural residents with limited access to transport and complements scheduled bus and rail services by facilitating onward connections. SmartPass holders can also receive free or discounted travel on Translink services through the Concessionary Fares Scheme, and on Dial-a-Lift services through the Assisted Rural Travel Scheme.Given the differing statutory roles and operating models of the two sectors, my Department has not undertaken a formal assessment of greater collaboration between Translink and Community Transport providers. However, I continue to support accessibility through increased funding for Community Transport programmes in 2025-26 and ongoing engagement with both sectors as part of wider work to improve access to public transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the reason why the review of Ulsterbus services, being undertaken by Translink, and the review of community transport services, being undertaken by her Department, are being conducted separately; and (ii) whether any consideration has been given to undertaking these reviews in a more integrated manner.
answer_text: The Translink Ulsterbus and the Community Transport reviews were commissioned to undertake specific objectives. While there are some touch points between the two reviews there is not sufficient overlap in objectives to warrant integrating the two pieces of work.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been made of the impact of the proposed arc21 incinerator on Lough Neagh, in light of the consultation response from the Water Management Unit.
answer_text: My department is a statutory consultee within the planning process and was consulted on this proposed development. A response to the information provided in an Environmental Statement dated 17 September 2025, was issued by Water Catchment Unit in relation to those areas which fall within their remit. This response highlighted concerns about drainage and construction issues associated with the proposed site.  Impacts on European sites such as Lough Neagh are assessed by The Shared Environmental Service on behalf of the Council.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any representations he has made to HMRC on behalf of childcare providers that face losing the 10 per cent wear and tear allowance under the new Making Tax Digital for Income Tax system.
To ask the Assembly Commission to outline any steps it is taking to improve public engagement and understanding of the Assembly’s work.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the timeline for lifting the Bluetongue Temporary Control Zone.
answer_text: You will be aware that the Bluetongue (BTV) Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) in Northern Ireland (NI) was lifted at 09:00 on Friday 06 March 2026.  This will mean that all animals, will be free to move within Northern Ireland.  The requirement for pre-movement testing of high-risk animals out of the TCZ for movements into wider NI will cease in line with the lifting of the zone.  		This decision has been made in line with DAERA’s Bluetongue Disease Control Framework which indicates that lifting of zones will be considered based on the available epidemiological and scientific evidence. 	My officials have also worked in close collaboration with industry stakeholders who are in favor of the easement of restrictions due to removal of the zone. 	Further information was also announced on 5 March concerning the application of an EU derogation, alongside the Republic of Ireland, to once again permit moves of susceptible animals into Northern Ireland from EU Member States for breeding and production.My officials will continue to ensure that our approach to BTV is proportionate to the risk and announcements about any further changes will be supported by clear communication from my department to key stakeholders and wider industry. Further information on these announcements and details on requirements for animal movements both into and outside of Northern Ireland, where required, is available on the DAERA website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any analysis and response his Department has made of (i) the 2024 Autism NI petition calling for a specialist Autism Mental Health Service for Northern Ireland; and (ii) local and international validated research on the tragic consequences of failure to provide appropriate interventions and support for autistic individuals living with mental health challenges.
answer_text: Since the launch of the Autism NI petition in 2024, there has been ongoing discussion between my Department, Autism NI and the All Party Group for Autism on this matter.  Whilst it is fair to say that autistic people could be served better by our mental health system, this does not sufficiently make the case for a specialist mental health service.It is acknowledged that often interactions with general mental health services are more difficult than they should be for autistic people.  The Mental Health Strategy 2021 - 2031 actively seeks to reduce barriers to accessing mental health services and supports.  This should include the provision of reasonable adjustments and ensuring mental health services are fully equipped to identify needs and support this client group.My Officials have undertaken a review of published guidelines and engaged with professional colleagues across NICE, Royal College of Psychiatrists, the British Psychological Society, NHS England and Royal College of Nursing, the professional consensus is clear: mainstream mental health services must be adapted, not bypassed.Training is key to ensure that appropriate adjustments are made for each autistic person, and to ensure that our mental health service is person-centered, adapting treatment and care at every point throughout a person’s journey.  Additional specialist training for staff working in the area of mental health is currently being developed in partnership with Middletown Centre for Autism.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale behind his Department not adopting the model of Autism Champions for Mental Health Services, to reinforce training take up, delivery and skill enhancement following the presentation to his officials from the All-Party Group on Autism.
answer_text: As part of recent discussions between the Department, Autism NI and the All-Party Group for Autism, the Department has been exploring further proposals to spread and scale a model of Autism Champions for Mental Health Services across all HSC Trusts. In considering this suggestion, it is noted that Autism Coordinators are already in place in each Health and Social Care Trust, providing leadership and support in this area. In addition, it is noted that our strategic approach, as set out in the Mental Health Strategy, the Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework and the Autism Strategy, is around building neuro-diversity awareness and understanding across all services and specialisms. It is not work that can easily be delivered through defined champion roles in each Trust but instead involves collective leadership and system-wide responsibility to disperse knowledge, skills and experience across all programmes of care across the lifespan. We are actively working to enhance inclusivity and enhance skills, knowledge and understanding across HSC through rolling out entry level neurodiversity awareness training and supplementing this with more specific, targeted training and the development of expertise to those professional roles where this is most impactful. Work is continuing to explore and scope different Autism Champion models currently used within HSC Trusts and in other jurisdictions, to compare approaches and identify examples of positive practice.  It is hoped that this work will be completed shortly and my officials will advise the All-Party Group of their recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) on the marketing strategy designed to promote the recently launched autism modules for health and social care staff; (ii) any costs incurred; (iii) any communication mediums used, including how this impacted the uptake of this training; and (iv) the number of staff that have completed the training module programme, broken down by (a) service location; and (b) profession, including the number that work in the mental health sector.
answer_text: My Department continues to promote uptake across HSC through AD forums, HSCT ASD Coordinators, and Trust Quality & Inclusion teams. Promotion has been planned through the Department and HSCT social media and all staff communication platforms.  The training is also being promoted by Middletown Centre and has been promoted and endorsed by the Independent Autism Reviewer. The cost to develop the training was £42,250. There has been no further cost incurred in promoting the training other than staff time. The training was promoted through Departmental social media with a Ministerial endorsement in December 2025. The Department press office has supported the promotion of the training across individual HSCT social media and all staff communication platforms. At this point the Department is unable to monitor the impact of promotional activity on the uptake of training. The number of staff that have completed the training module programme, broken down by (a) service location; and (b) profession, including the number that work in the mental health sector is set out below.The training takes approximately 3 hours to complete and the number of staff who had completed all 3 modules of the training by location at 1st February 2026 was; Service Location Staff BHSCT62BSO - SPPG7NHSCT139NIAS2Public Health Agency2RQIA1SEHSCT100SHSCT57WHSCT127Total 497It is currently not possible to identify staff by profession. Mental health and learning disability staff are reported through HSCT identified Directorates and cannot currently be reported specifically by mental health service. The number of staff working in mental health and learning disability directorates is 158.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the impact that severe weather has on health for those in chronic homelessness, to detail any work (i) his Department; and (ii) it's arm's-length bodies, is doing to support the Housing Executive when the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol is triggered.
answer_text: Following a meeting with the Welcome Organisation during September 2025, my Department agreed to convene a cross departmental meeting to help ensure a co-ordinated response during the winter period for the homeless population.  The meeting was held on 5 November 2025, with representatives from the Department of Health, Department of Justice, Department for Communities, the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, and the Public Health Agency.  It included a discussion on the triggering of a Severe Weather Emergency Protocol (SWEP).A further Homeless Winter Preparedness meeting was held on 26 January 2026, and it is envisaged that another meeting will be held in the Spring of 2026 to consider our response during this winter period and to prepare for next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) processes in place; and (ii) support available, from hospitals when a person experiencing chronic homelessness is discharged to ensure that they are properly supported in the community.
answer_text: When a person experiencing chronic homelessness is being discharged from hospital they can be referred to Hospital Social Work services. Following receipt of referral, a holistic assessment is undertaken which considers a broad range of issues including housing status, safety, finances, health needs and available community-based support networks. These teams work closely with homelessness services to explore emergency, temporary or longer-term accommodation options.  The Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) acts as the statutory homelessness authority, therefore staff are required to liaise with NIHE when someone presents as homeless or at risk of homelessness.  In respect of children and young people, should a young person present to hospital and reports that they are experiencing homelessness, there are several potential pathways for support in relation to accommodation and any assessed needs.   Where the young person has been in care (a Looked After Child), they will be referred to the appropriate social work service such as a 16+ team or a Leaving and After Care Service for immediate action and identification of support in meeting their needs including accommodation support.   Where the young person has not previously been a Looked After Child, is aged 16/17 and presents as homeless or is referred to the Trust as they are deemed to be at immediate risk of homelessness, this will be considered an emergency.  Action will then be taken immediately based on presenting need and an initial assessment of the child’s needs will be undertaken.  Services will be identified, based on assessed need, which will provide holistic support including, where necessary, the provision of accommodation and consideration, as required to the young person being accommodated as a Child in Care.   Subject to the assessment, the child will be offered bespoke, ongoing support, according to the assessment of their needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how her Department intends to strengthen protections for women and girls exploited through adult services websites.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any immediate provision that will be made to ensure flood protection for the residents of Antrim town.
To ask the Minister of Health why Dexcom G7 continuous glucose monitoring is not available across all Health and Social Care Trusts for children with type 1 diabetes.
answer_text: Dexcom G7 sensor is a type of complex Continuous Glucose monitoring device for monitoring blood glucose levels. It can integrate as part of an insulin pump therapy system to enable Hybrid Closed Loop technology to automatically regulate insulin delivery, according to the glucose level. The most appropriate insulin pump therapy system is determined in consultation between the clinician and the patient and/or the patient’s carer, based on their clinical need. Continuous glucose monitors currently listed on the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff can be provided.  As Dexcom G7 sensors are currently not listed on the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff, funding for these devices has to come directly from the local Trust, in line with clinical need, or through self-funding.All five Trusts currently have children and young people living with type 1 diabetes who are using Dexcom G7 sensors, either funded by the local Trust, funded at risk by individual services or self-funded. The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence Technical Appraisal 943 which recommends Hybrid Closed Loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in type 1 diabetes, has been endorsed as applicable to Northern Ireland.  My officials within SPPG have supported the development of a five-year implementation plan through a clinical working group with relevant stakeholders across Health and Social Care.  Funding has been secured to support the implementation of this plan through the Regional Insulin Pump service, led by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in partnership with the remaining Trusts.  The plan is in the final stages of drafting and will support equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will implement reasonable adjustments for autistic individuals within the mental health service to ensure they are heard, understood and supported appropriately, as is required under both the Disability Discrimination Act 1995 and the Autism Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: It is acknowledged that often interactions with general mental health services are more difficult than they should be for autistic people. The Mental Health Strategy 2021 to 2031 recognises that there are barriers currently preventing this client group, and other marginalised groups, from accessing mental health services and support. It therefore actively seeks to reduce barriers and to implement a “no wrong door” approach to access to services, ensuring staff are appropriately trained to identify and address the specific needs of marginalised or at-risk groups, and ensuring that services can be flexible to meet individual needs at the point of contact.   It is important that mental health services are fully equipped to identify the specific needs of individuals and address these appropriately, whether through engagement with support services, onward referral to specialist services or interventions if required, or by the employment of innovative solutions such as digital mental health interventions.  Training is key to ensure that appropriate adjustments are made for each autistic person, and to ensure that our mental health service is person-centered, adapting treatment and care at every point throughout a person’s journey.   Health and Social Care staff have access to a range of autism training modules and work has recently been developed by my Department in partnership with the Middletown Centre for Autism as an internationally recognised Autism Centre of Excellence. The new modules cover Autistic Differences, Providing Supportive Environments and Promoting a Neuro-affirming Culture. This training has been co-produced with service users. Over forty percent of the training is delivered by neurodivergent people describing their lived experience. This training provides high quality foundational knowledge helping staff to understand and implement reasonable adjustments. Additional specialist training for staff working in the area of mental health is currently being developed in partnership with Middletown Centre for Autism.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how the establishment of the Office of AI and Digital will change how services are delivered across government.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the progress he is making in bringing forward the next phase of the Active Ageing Strategy.
answer_text: Progress in bringing forward the next phase of the Active Ageing Strategy Action Plan continues at pace, and my officials are engaging with colleagues from other Executive departments to assist in its development. The next step will be to collate this information to prepare an initial draft of the Action Plan, setting out the planned activities and actions against the key priority areas.A pre-consultation stakeholder engagement process is expected to begin once we have collated the returns from all Departments and produced an initial draft of the Action Plan.Once this work has been completed, I will share a draft of the Action Plan with my Executive colleagues for their consideration and seeking agreement to proceed to the consultation stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when an aviation strategy will be published.
answer_text: My Department has been working closely with stakeholders in the aviation sector in the development of a draft aviation policy. A sub-group of the Tourism Partnership Board which consists of representatives from the three local airports as well as key tourism stakeholders was also established to advise on policy proposals. Public consultation on the aviation policy is expected to begin in March.This consultation aims to gather feedback on a wide range of issues currently impacting our aviation sector. These include maintaining and improving connectivity, fostering inward tourism and investment, enhancing skills and training and addressing sustainability, to ensure the future policy accurately reflects the needs and priorities of all stakeholders involved.

To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the number of small and medium-sized enterprises that currently benefit from business rates relief in South Antrim.
answer_text: My Department does not record business rate relief by parliamentary constituency as the rating system operates on a district council and local wards basis. The South Antrim Constituency primarily covers the Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council area.  Rate support is provided to around 2,600 businesses in that District Council who are currently receiving support of just over £10 million through Small Business Rate Relief, Industrial Derating, Non-Domestic Vacant Rating exclusions and Back in Business Rate Relief.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the current waiting list for patients awaiting a consultant-led outpatient appointment in South Antrim.
answer_text: My Department does not collect information requested at constituency level. However, given the constituency is largely in the Northern Trust, I am able to provide the latest quarterly figures: as at 30 September 2025, 92,466 patients were waiting on a first consultant led outpatient appointment in the Northern Trust.The revised Elective Care Framework, which was published on 24 May 2024, sets out a five-year roadmap to reduce waiting times, including how earmarked funding would be used for waiting list activity. As you are aware, only £50m of this funding represents new money. However, even with these financial pressures, there has been significant progress with the number of patients waiting over four years for an outpatient assessment reduced by 35% from April to November 2025. Funding has been invested across a range of initiatives to support waiting list reduction and strengthen core capacity and my Department is currently working with the Northern Trust to expand general surgery capacity on the Causeway site, which will not only benefit Northern Trust residents but also the wider HSC. Additional non-recurrent funding has also been provided to support the ongoing provision of mega clinics this year and this service model is having a positive impact on reducing the number of people waiting for assessment in Northern Ireland. Funding has also been provided to expand pre-operative assessment capacity across all Trusts which will help ensure that patients are optimised for surgery, reducing the risk of short notice cancellations.I hope that the information provided gives some reassurance that there are plans in place to address the overall waiting lists, not only for patients in South Antrim, but across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidance in place to support GPs in managing adult ADHD following diagnosis, including any arrangements for shared care with independent sector clinicians.
answer_text: Individuals choosing to procure healthcare privately are effectively stepping outside of the health and social care (HSC) system.  In these circumstances, there is no obligation on GPs to provide pre and/or post treatment support.  Therefore, it remains important for individuals wishing to avail of private healthcare to ensure that all pre and post treatment support costs are included in the overall package of care procured from the private healthcare provider.  In instances where any ongoing treatment and medication is recommended, but is not provided, by the private provider as part of the private service then individuals should be prepared to meet any associated costs.Where a GP is made aware of an individual’s plans to procure private healthcare in a private capacity, it is recommended that: the individual is encouraged to ensure all pre and post treatment costs are included in the overall package.the individual is informed that they may have to pay for any ongoing treatment or medications which are recommended.As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care, and management of their individual patients. They remain responsible for clinical decisions regarding the care of individual patients and as such they should exercise their clinical judgement prior to providing any pre and/or post treatment support for individuals availing of private healthcare.In specific circumstances, where a GP is being asked to provide an HSC prescription to a patient based on the recommendation of a private practitioner, there may be additional factors to consider. The legacy Health and Social Care Board issued correspondence on a number of occasions to private providers and general practice, most recently in June 2021, regarding requests for HSC prescriptions following private consultation.  The purpose of this correspondence was to remind both sectors of their clinical responsibilities in relation to prescribing of medications, including Red-Amber list drugs, to patients under their care. In particular, the limitations with regards to requests to GPs for transfer of ongoing prescribing of medication which for the patient’s GP may not be clinically acceptable.  This guidance has not changed.	While there is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Northern Ireland, a private sector ADHD diagnosis does not preclude GPs entering into shared arrangements.  However, it remains at their discretion.To facilitate appropriate shared care arrangements, my Department issued ‘Private (non-HSC) Prescribing Requests Guidance for Primary Care in September 2024. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting times for adult ADHD assessments, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Northern Ireland.  ADHD provision has evolved across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams.  There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but there are inconsistencies and lengthy waiting times across Trusts.In response, the Department commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. The ADHD needs assessment report was received by the Department at the end of November 2025 and is currently being considered by officials, with a view to identifying next steps and arranging publication thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how his Department intends to monitor and enforce compliance with livestock movement restrictions in any bluetongue control area; and (ii) which agencies will be responsible for enforcement.
answer_text: (i) Livestock movement restrictions already exist in Northern Ireland for diseases such as bovine TB and Avian Influenza and so mechanisms for monitoring and enforcement are well established and trusted.(ii) Veterinary Service Animal Health Group is responsible for enforcement in relation to Bluetongue restrictions. Any non-compliances will be investigated by my Department’s Welfare and Enforcement branch.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he plans to introduce mandatory bluetongue vaccination within any restriction or surveillance zones; and (ii) to detail any assessment made of vaccine supply, cost implications and rollout capacity.
answer_text: At this stage, I do not plan to introduce mandatory bluetongue vaccination.  The supply of BTV-3 vaccines to Northern Ireland and the supply of veterinary medicines more widely are matters under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.I authorised the voluntary use of three bluetongue serotype 3 (BTV-3) vaccines in Northern Ireland, subject to licensing, from July 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to issue further statements to the Assembly on the suspected bluetongue case, given the fast moving nature of the situation and the need for timely updates to the farming community.
answer_text: As detailed in my statement to the Assembly on Monday 1 December, I will ensure updates are provided, as necessary, to my Executive colleagues and also, importantly, to the AERA Committee, in the first instance. I will be happy to return to the Assembly if there are any further significant developments in the time ahead in addition to my appearance at the AERA Committee on 4 December, alongside the Deputy Chief Veterinary Officer and Permanent Secretary where an update on the situation was provided for Members.As this issue develops at pace, my department will continue to issue timely press releases on all significant developments. In addition, my officials are keeping affected farmers and wider industry regularly updated with timely communications. This includes convening stakeholder meetings, direct communications to affected livestock keepers within the disease control zones in the form of text messages, emails and letters, as well as regular updates to the DAERA website. Any updates to this developing situation can be found at: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures in place to prevent farmers, contractors and personnel within a restriction zone from attending livestock markets or undertaking farm-to-farm movements that may risk spreading bluetongue.
answer_text: Disease control measures, including certain movement restrictions, have been applied to those farms within the Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) to mitigate the risk of onward disease spread. Any non-compliances of the necessary movement restrictions will be taken seriously and investigated by my Department’s Welfare and Enforcement branch for breaches of the legislation and possible enforcement action.	Any further updates regarding movement restrictions will be communicated directly to all stakeholders where appropriate and will be available on the DAERA website at: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans he has to support farmers whose businesses have been disrupted as a result of bluetongue movement restrictions, including any future vaccination requirements.
answer_text: From Tuesday 9 December 2025 moves into the Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) have been permitted on a General Licence, available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/bluetongue-general-licences In addition, moves from the free area within Northern Ireland (NI) to Great Britain (GB) are now also permitted with no requirements beyond those which normally apply.  Those who wish to export livestock to GB should contact their private veterinary practitioner in the normal manner.For further information please consult https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/topics/exporting-animals-and-animal-products-northern-ireland-gb-eu-and-third-countries-including-guidance-and-application-point-daera-export-certificate-system-decs.Any further updates regarding movement restrictions will be communicated directly to all stakeholders where appropriate and will be available on the DAERA website at: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-irelandDisease control zones are implemented as a disease control response measure and my department has undertaken extensive surveillance and testing, aimed at mitigating against any further disease spread and protect the Bluetongue free area, unaffected farms within the restricted area and the wider Northern Ireland agri-economy. My department has no plans to provide funding support for Bluetongue vaccine rollout in Northern Ireland at this time.  It is not mandatory to have your livestock vaccinated for BTV-3, it is a decision for the livestock keeper to make in conjunction with advice from their private veterinarian.  It should be noted that BTV vaccines do not provide cross protection for other serotypes of the virus.  For further information, including any Bluetongue updates and helpful information on BTV-3 vaccination please visit: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department’s current priorities for improving road safety on rural and high-risk routes across Northern Ireland.

To ask the Assembly Commission to outline any measures being taken to strengthen the Assembly’s cyber security and digital resilience, particularly in light of the rise in cyber attacks on public bodies across the UK and Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any discussions he and his officials have had with the Department for the Economy in relation to the re-establishment of the Inter-Departmental Biomethane Group.
answer_text: Officials in my Department have been working with Department for the Economy officials to re-establish the Inter-Departmental Biomethane Group (IDBG) with the first meeting having taken place on 6 November. Discussions are focused on ensuring the group’s purpose reflects the impact of biomethane production within the energy sector, with an emphasis on sustainability. Officials from both Departments have agreed on the need for increased clear strategic direction to support investment and development in the local biomethane sector. The group will include DAERA and DfE officials with additional government and industry representatives included in future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will provide copies of findings from any (i) Education and Training Inspectorate; or (ii) Education Authority, inspections or reviews, which identify transport gaps or challenges in post-16 and post-19 special educational needs and disabilities provision, in the North Eastern area.
answer_text: The EA has advised that it does not hold this information.The ETI does not report on gaps and challenges in specific geographical areas.  District Inspectors would be aware of challenges through their district work; district findings inform phase insights reports.  ETI is currently carrying out, on behalf of the Department for the Economy, an evaluation of provision in Northern Ireland’s further education colleges for students with special educational needs and/or disabilities (SEN/D).  A report of the evaluation findings will be published on the ETI website in 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of 16 to 25 year olds who currently have (i) a statement of special educational needs; and (ii) an Education, Health and Care Plan, within the North Eastern Education Authority area.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised the total number of children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust from age 16 -19 years is 1,020. The provision of education for a child with SEN is set out in the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 Part II Article 3, which states that for the purposes of the provision of special education a ‘child’ is defined as ‘any person who has not attained the age of nineteen years and is a registered pupil at a school.’The provisions set out in the Children’s and Families Act 2014, including Education, Health and Care Plans, extend to England and Wales only; they do not extend to Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any current (i) waiting lists; (ii) unmet demand; or (iii) capacity pressures, affecting 16 to 25 year olds with special educational needs who are unable to access educational placements, due to (a) transport barriers; or (b) a lack of suitable local provision, in the North Eastern region.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:There are no known barriers at present which is preventing the EA from assessing and providing transport assistance for pupils and students as required.The EA provide transport assistance for pupils with a Statement of Special Education Need who have an identified special transport need up to the age of 19.The EA also applies the DE Transport Circular on behalf of the Department for the Economy for students attending FE Colleges who are under 19 years of age at the beginning of the academic year in which transport assistance is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any feedback gathered by (a) his Department; (b) the Education Authority; or (c) partner agencies; from (d) parents; (e) schools; or (f) support services, regarding transport barriers faced by 16 to 25 year olds with special educational needs; and (ii) the impact of those barriers on participation in (a) education; (b) training; and (c) community activities.
answer_text: No specific efforts have been made to gather feedback on this matter.The Education Authority (EA) has advised they provide transport assistance for school pupils in accordance with the Department of Education Home to School Transport Policy, and this includes transport provision for pupils with a statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN) who have additional transport needs requiring more bespoke transport. In addition, the EA provides transport assistance for eligible students attending Further Education colleges who are under 19 years of age at the beginning of the academic year. Eligibility for transport assistance and the assessment of any additional SEN transport needs is determined according to the individual circumstances of the pupil and transport is arranged accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on TransformEd NI.
answer_text: The TransformED Reform Programme is driving a bold, ambitious and system-wide transformation of education.At the heart of TransformED is the need to equip and empower our educators. This is demonstrated by the engagement of 161 post-primary schools and over 7,000 teachers benefiting from the Online CPD Academy. In addition, the £31 million Teacher Professional Learning Fund is enabling schools to deliver high-quality, evidence-based development tailored to their needs, while the new Initial Teacher Education (ITE) Bursary Scheme is addressing critical subject shortages.Research-informed practice is being embedded through initiatives such as the Science of Learning newsletters, teacher-edited publications, and over 50 practitioner-led conferences.  Much needed curriculum reform is advancing through the establishment of the Curriculum Taskforce and subject working groups.  My ambition is to have a new framework developed for consultation in the Spring.  Assessment and qualifications reform is underway, with pilots of Computer Adaptive Assessment in 522 schools and a system-level sample assessment in literacy and numeracy. A public consultation on GCSE, AS, and A-level reform aims to reduce exam burden and enhance meaningful learning.Legislative progress is being made on inspection reform and raising the participation age to 18.   The RAISE programme continues to be rolled out across Northern Ireland, with innovative projects such as Reading with AI.As we move through the academic year, our strategic focus is on deepening, accelerating, and scaling implementation. Key priorities include supporting schools in accessing professional learning, advancing curriculum reform, progressing qualifications and legislative changes, and expanding evidence-based initiatives to close the attainment gap and ensure every child can succeed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department plans to ensure that reducing the number of exam papers does not result in single papers carrying greater weighting toward final grades.
answer_text: The consultation on The Future of CCEA GCSEs, AS Levels and A Levels is a key element of my TransformED Strategy and is an important step in reviewing the policy framework that underpins CCEA qualifications. After the consultation closes and all views have been carefully considered alongside other empirical evidence, I intend to confirm my policy framework intentions in early 2026.  This will then inform the revision of CCEA’s qualifications.  It is from that point in time, if I have taken the decision to reduce the number of exam papers, that considerations such as weighting towards final grades will be explored by CCEA.I hope you find this information helpful

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment that has been made on the potential impact on disadvantaged pupils, or those with special educational needs, that could arise from reducing the number of exams students are required to sit, given that some pupils may benefit from multiple assessment opportunities rather than one or two high-stakes exams.
answer_text: My proposals to reduce content and limit the number of assessments are intended to reduce the ongoing pressure of repeated assessments.   Currently, many Northern Ireland students sit high stakes exams annually from Year 11 to Year 14.  This is uncommon practice internationally, where exams typically occur only at the end of compulsory education, usually at age 18.I am interested to hear all views before I make any final decisions. My officials have therefore engaged with a range of stakeholders, including school leaders, young people and parents.After the consultation closes and all views have been carefully considered alongside other empirical evidence, I intend to confirm my policy framework intentions in early 2026.  This will then inform the revision of CCEA’s qualifications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has assessed the impact on deaf service users of having to rely on written communication during safeguarding, care planning or assessment processes.
answer_text: Across Northern Ireland, support for deaf service users and their families is delivered by the five Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts in partnership with the community and voluntary sector in accordance with assessed need. My Department recognises the many challenges that are faced by individuals who are deaf or hard of hearing. At this time, my Department has not undertaken a formal assessment of the impact on deaf service users of having to rely upon written communication during safeguarding, care planning or assessment processes.  The HSC Trusts currently employ the use of the Sign Language Interaction (SLI) service to communicate with the deaf population. Sign Language Interactions, is the sole provider for the Regional Health and Social Care Communication Support Service for People who are Deaf, deafblind and Hard of Hearing. SLI offer a full range of communication supports, 24/7 including:Face to Face Sign Language InterpretingRemote Sign Language InterpretingRelay InterpretersInterpreters for deaf blind peopleLip speakersElectronic Note takersSpeech to Text ReportingIn the course of availing of support through SLI, deaf service users are offered opportunities to ask follow up questions or to ensure that the information being shared has been fully understood.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidance issued to Health and Social Care Trusts regarding communication support for deaf service users who cannot communicate effectively in written English.
answer_text: There are a number of training opportunities, awareness raising sessions and associated material made available to health and social care staff in relation to the Regional Communication Support Service (RCSS) for communicating with d/Deaf individuals.  It is recognised that circumstances may arise where written English is an individual’s preferred method of communication, however, even whereby an individual can communicate effectively in written English, this should not be a substitute for face to face or video remote BSL or ISL interpreting, if BSL and ISL is the individual’s first language.  HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland have worked collaboratively on the development of their new five-year Disability Action Plan and Equality Action Plan (2024-2029).  These plans outline actions and initiatives intended to address section 75 inequalities, improve equality of opportunity, promote positive attitudes for disabled people and improve their participation in public life.  A specific Action is included in the 2024 – 2029 Plan in relation to d/Deaf and Hard of Hearing service users.  This commits all Trusts to continue the roll out of the RCSS for People who are d/Deaf, d/Deafblind and Hard of Hearing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of social workers currently trained in (i) British Sign Language; and (ii) Irish Sign Language, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) In the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, within the Sensory Support Team, there are eight (8) Social Workers trained in British Sign Language (BSL) to Level 2. Additionally, several team members have undertaken further BSL training to Levels 4 and 6 to enhance communication and service provision for individuals with hearing loss.(ii)At present, none of the Social Workers within the Sensory Support Team are trained in Irish Sign Language (ISL). This is due to a limited demand for ISL within the Trust’s service user population. However, the Trust maintains a contractual arrangement with Sign Language Interactions (SLI), who provide 24-hour, 7-days-a-week access to both BSL and ISL interpreters to ensure equitable communication support for all service users when required.(i)Within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust Sensory Support Team there are two (2) Social Workers trained in British Sign Language (BSL) to level 1; six (6) Social Workers trained in BSL to Level 2 and one (1) Social Worker trained to Level 3.(ii)The Trust have no Social Work staff trained in Irish Sign Language (ISL). (i)In the Southern Trust seven (7) Social Workers in the Sensory Support Team have trained in BSL to level 2.(ii)The Trust have no Social Work staff trained in Irish Sign Language (ISL). (i & ii)The Northern Health and Social Care Trust and the Western Health and Social Care Trust have advised that they do not hold the relevant information pertaining to these questions and therefore are not able to report. In addition to the Social Work staff trained in the specified Trust areas, I can confirm that the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within my Department commission a regional communication contract for individuals who are d/Deaf and d/Deafblind.  This contract provides sign language interpreters both on a face to face basis and remotely via video link across 24 hours a day, 7 days per week.  The interpreters are accessed via the Health and Social Care staff for all appointments and communications with individuals who use British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language as their first language.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is taking to increase the number of Health and Social Care Trust staff trained in (i) British Sign Language; and (ii) Irish Sign Language, to support deaf service users and care-home residents.
answer_text: As of June 2025, my Department recorded over 75,000 employees in the health and social care sector in Northern Ireland.  Whilst it is acknowledged that particular service areas within HSC Trusts may be more likely to come into contact with individuals whose first language is BSL or ISL, the ability to ensure that staff in these numbers are trained in sign language to the level required and to maintain their skills through using BSL or ISL on a regular basis, would prove very challenging.  It is important to note that there are 6 levels of training in BSL and ISL and  the ability to function at level 6 would be the standard required in health and social care to ensure an ability to interpret complex medical and social care terminology.  As such, the Regional Communication Support Service (RCSS) commissioned by my Department, is available to health and social care staff as a means of ensuring an appropriate level of support and enhanced safety for d/Deaf individuals when communicating with health and social care professionals.  This facility does not detract from the recognised value of increasing usage of BSL and ISL and promoting awareness and fluency amongst staff. Within all Trusts, and particularly within sensory support teams, there are a number of staff members trained at various levels of BSL. Whilst this is an important and valuable aide to support communication with d/Deaf individuals, it is recognised that when facilitating complex conversations relating to an individual’s health and social care needs, this should be undertaken with the support of a fully qualified interpreter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any funding has been allocated in the current financial year to improve access to British and Irish Sign Language interpreting within Health and Social Care Trust services and care homes.
answer_text: There has been no new, additional funding allocated in this financial year. However, there has been a continuation of recurrent funding to ensure access to British Sign Language (BSL) and Irish Sign Language (ISL) interpreting within Health and Social Care Trust services and care homes.  My Department commissions the contract for the Regional Communication Support Service (RCSS) which is provided by Sign Language Interactions (SLI).  The RCSS is a service for people who are d/Deaf, d/Deafblind, or hard of hearing and require an interpreter when accessing health and social care services.  Requests for BSL or ISL interpreting in line with the criteria for this contract have in no way been limited or restricted due to finances, since the commencement of this contract.  All requests are actioned and met, with the exception of when an appropriate interpreter is not available on a face to face basis for a specific date and time requested.  In such circumstances, alternatives are offered and remote interpreting is also available 24 hours per day, 7 days per week.  SPPG have convened an Advisory Board to support with continued guidance from key agencies including d/Deaf individuals in order to hold to account and continue to improve the RCSS for the d/Deaf community in Northern Ireland.My Department along with key partners from HSC Trusts, community and voluntary sector organisations and independent sector agencies, are currently focused upon improving education for both health and social care staff and d/Deaf and d/Deafblind individuals utilising the RCSS and upon ensuring harmonised regional approaches in relation to booking interpreting services.  The resources and services outlined below are examples of the support on offer to health and social care staff; Staff posters, with QR codes are displayed in Trust staff areas outlining how to book a BSL/ISL interpreter.  Trusts also share guidance and reminders to all staff on how to book an interpreter through various forums including; global staff emails, staff newsletters and desktop computer screensavers.Posters relating to the service are available to be displayed in all public facing Trust facilities/service areas to increase service user awareness.SLI have appointed Community Liaison Officers who provide service awareness training to both the d/Deaf community and HSC staff on booking and accessing interpreters.   The British Deaf Association are contracted by my Department to provide Deaf Awareness training for HSC staff, with this training including guidance around how to book a BSL/ISL interpreter.  Over 400 HSC staff have availed of this training in the last 3 years.Dedicated equality leads are established in all Trusts.There has also been development and dissemination of an online “accessible and inclusive communication toolkit” for staff, which incorporates guidance on how to book a sign language interpreter.The dissemination and promotion of videos raising awareness of the importance of the nee

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when she will announce the approval of the pay award for police officers for 2025-26, in line with the recommendations of the Police Remuneration Review Body.
To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any differentiation in the nature of support her Department provides to innocent victims and the perpetrators of crime for accessing justice via the courts.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of current NHS (i) GP; and (ii) dental, provision in Ballyclare in relation to the population growth in the town over the last decade.
answer_text: (i) I fully recognise the pivotal role that General Practice plays within our health and social care system. The increase in population in Northern Ireland has led to increased demand on health services.  I can assure you that my Department is continuing to take measures, both in the short and longer term, to secure improved sustainability of General Practice provision and ensure better support for the delivery of these vitally important services in Ballyclare and throughout Northern Ireland.With regards to funding provided by my Department in this financial year, the total investment in GP services will be more than £414m. This includes pay uplifts from last year, and the recently announced investment in MDTs, which will go to directly supporting GPs in meeting the needs of their patients by expanding capacity in practices.The Primary Care Multidisciplinary Team (MDT) programme which is population based is key to stabilising and strengthening vital Primary Care services to ensure they can continue to provide high quality care to our people, now and into the future.  The MDT Programme Team in SPPG work closely with relevant stakeholders on the development and roll out of MDTS, however each local area has autonomy to decide on how the plans will be implemented at a local level. The MDT programme will be expanded to a further five areas in which the East Antrim area is included and therefore the Ballyclare area will also benefit.My Department has also announced investment of £2.9m in GP Elective Care, which will benefit GPs directly while meeting the needs of patients.(ii) With regards to General Dental Services, I am aware that some patients, particularly those not currently registered with a dentist are finding it more difficult to obtain an appointment with a Health Service dentist in Ballyclare and throughout Northern Ireland.Under the current General Dental Service arrangements, the Department does not have a role in determining the location of dental practices. General Dental Practitioners are independent contractors, and the location of new practices is a business decision made by practice owners.In an effort to improve access for all patients, I approved an additional £7m investment in General Dental Services in 2025/26. This investment is funding the continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme and 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions, and root canals, alongside a new £1.6m allocation to provide additional support to dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care for their patients. The two dental practices in Ballyclare which provide health service dentistry will benefit from all three elements of this investment.In parallel the Dental Access Scheme continues to provide access for unregistered patients with an urgent or pressing oral health need. The nearest participating practices to Ballyclare include Antrim, Larne, and Newtownabbey.Longer term, my Department

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures being taken to reduce the number of patients being denied access to preventative care services, such as treatment room testing, without prior GP authorisation.
answer_text: Preventative care can relate to many services including screening, weight management, smoking cessation, alcohol related harm, cholesterol and hypertension screening among many others. Care navigators within Primary Care are trained to signpost any patient to the correct healthcare professional in their team depending on individual practice protocols.  For this reason, most treatment room testing is facilitated and authorised by a healthcare professional.Depending on their size individual Primary Care treatment rooms may be staffed with a range of Nurses and Healthcare Assistants who also have different levels of training. A patient may not be familiar with this and booking an appointment with the wrong healthcare professional could result in wasted appointments at a time when Primary Care and treatments rooms are under considerable pressure.  Authorisation of testing ensures that testing is justified, avoids over testing and facilitates the efficient use of scarce resources.Any testing required is usually linked to a clinical query or concern and GPs remain responsible for ensuring that testing is appropriate and safe for the patient.   The GP must also be in a position to interpret the result of requested tests in the context of the patient’s medical history, medications and wider clinical picture, and prior authorisation enables safe clinical practice.For the reasons set out above it would be unwise for patients to directly order their own blood tests with a treatment room. The authorisation process requires each test to carry a GP cypher number and a GP’s permission is required in order to use this. It is a clinical decision as to which tests are required for an individual patient and the clinician who requests a test is responsible for receiving and reviewing the results, and determining an action plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans in place to expand NHS GP practices in Ballyclare to ensure patients can access timely appointments.
answer_text: My Department acknowledges the very real pressures facing General Practice and is aware of the continuing challenges facing General Practice and the impact that increasing demand and workload pressures are having on GPs including those in the Ballyclare area.I can assure you that my Department is continuing to take measures, both in the short and longer term, to secure improved sustainability of General Practice provision and ensure better support for the delivery of these vitally important services in Ballyclare and throughout Northern Ireland.My Department remains committed to supporting the long-term sustainability of general practice. Work is commencing to explore a new model for primary care, focusing on a neighbourhood and multidisciplinary approach to delivering care closer to home. We hope that GP representatives will engage in this work with us and clinical leaders across the system.The MDT model remains the key to stabilising and strengthening vial Primary Care services to ensure they can continue to provide high quality care to our people, now and into the future.I recently announced investment of £61 million in expanding Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Teams (MDTs) over the next four years which will support the completion of MDTs in the 7 existing areas where implementation has already commenced, with the expansion to a further 5 Federation areas including the East Antrim Federation area and therefore the Ballyclare area will also benefit. Detailed local plans have been developed between Trusts and GP Federations to determine the sequencing and timescale of investment in practices. Officials are working with local areas to finalise these plans, and I expect this to be complete in the coming weeks.In addition, the Ballyclare Group Practice submitted an improvement grant application in February 2024 to develop their existing premises at George Avenue, Ballyclare to accommodate MDT teams. The application has been approved in principle and planning approval was granted on 25 February 2025.Health Estates design review is complete and the practice professional advisors have advised to progress to the tender stage. SPPG funding for 2025/2026 has been prioritised to take the extension forward in financial year 2025/2026 and 2026/2027, subject to full business case approval and budgetary constraints.My Department also continues to focus on building the GP workforce and supporting recruitment and retention. The increase in GP training places to 121 per year will be maintained, despite pressures in the training budget. This represents an increase of 86% from 65 places in 2015/16.The GP Training Group, which includes the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency and GP representatives, will also progress work to project the number of GP training places required over the next 5 years. It is important to note however that the increase in demand for Primary Care services cannot be met solely by increasing the number of GPs. Rather this is part of a wid

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether additional funding or workforce recruitment is being allocated to Ballyclare to increase NHS (i) GP; and (ii) dental capacity, in response to the large number of new homes in the area.
answer_text: Despite the continuing challenging budgetary position, over the last 10 years, investment in General Practice has grown by 55.3% in cash terms and 18.4% in real terms.  Although there may not be specific plans for the Ballyclare area I can assure you my Department continues to focus on building the GP workforce and supporting recruitment and retention across Northern Ireland. The increase in GP training places to 121 per year will be maintained, despite pressures in the training budget. This represents an increase of 86% from 65 places in 2015/16.The GP Training Group, which includes the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency and GP representatives, will also progress work to project the number of GP training places required over the next 5 years.My Department has worked with key stakeholders to increase the exposure of undergraduates to General Practice; encourage more GP trainees; and ensure that there are sufficient GP training practices to meet demand and support GPs in training.There are a number of schemes in place to support GPs, including the Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme, the GP Retainer scheme, the GP Mentor and Fellowship schemes, and the Northern Ireland Induction and Refresher scheme (now known as the International Induction Programme and the Return to Practice Programme).The GP retainer scheme offers a flexible and supportive framework for experienced GPs who wish to continue practising without committing to full-time hours. This approach has been instrumental in retaining skilled professionals who might otherwise have left the profession due to work life balance considerations.The GP Mentor Scheme enables GPs who have been working in general practice in NI for some time, as well as those entering the workforce, to avail of support and mentorship from experienced, trained peers in a safe and positive environment.GP Fellowship Schemes are supporting recently qualified GPs to transition from the training environment to independent practice, with opportunities for personal and professional development.I can assure you that my Department is committed to stabilising pressurised GP services.Under the current General Dental Service arrangements, the Department does not have a role in determining the location of dental practices. General Dental Practitioners are independent contractors, and the location of new practices is a business decision made by practice owners. The provision of Health Service dentistry by any practitioners who chose to work in Ballyclare will be funded as part of the General Dental Service demand led budget. I approved a £7m investment in General Dental Services for 2025/2026  in May 2025 to continue supporting the profession while reform of the service is progressed. The investment in funding includes the continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme and 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions and root canals, alongside a new £1.6m allocation to provide additional su

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to prevent avoidable hospital admissions in Ballyclare, caused by delays in accessing primary care.
answer_text: As Primary Care providers, GP practices play a key role in preventing avoidable hospital admissions through proactive, co-ordinated and continuous care to patients.  The General Medical Services (GMS) core responsibilities, along with a broad range of Enhanced Services offered by many practices, enable GP practices to offer prevention, early detection and chronic disease management as part of their role. By way of examples, my Department makes available, to all GP practices, funding for services such as:Proactive GP Care for Nursing and Residential Homes - enabling GPs to visit, review and plan the ongoing care of residents in care homesGP Anticipatory Care Plan & Key Information Summaries funding practices, such as Ballyclare, to closely monitor and plan the care of patients with longterm conditions and those requiring palliative careThere are around 35 Enhanced Services all of which complement the day to day care provided by GPs, practice nurses and pharmacists and which are designed to promote health and wellbeing and so avoid admissions to hospital.I recognise that being able to access healthcare services when we need to do so is important for all of us.As independent contractors, GPs manage the day to day running of their practices, including the use of technology, telephony and management of their appointment system within the constraints of their available workforce.  However, my Department remains committed to ensuring that patients can continue to access high-quality, sustainable care within their communities.  Access is an integral part of the overall GP contract for 2025/26 and to assist, a series of access indicators will be incorporated into the Northern Ireland Assurance Framework (NICAF) which is the key governance and accountability mechanism for the GMS contract.  These access indicators aim to create small but significant improvement to the patient experience of access, to remove some of the friction points for patients that can make the experience worse for patients and practices alike.  The introduction of these measures aims to improve access for patients, all helping to avoid hospital admissions, in Ballyclare and across N Ireland, and which is a key priority for my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to list all road resurfacing schemes which are anticipated to be completed within Antrim and Newtownabbey District by the end of the present financial year.
answer_text: I am pleased to be able to confirm the following schemes within the Antrim and Newtownabbey area are scheduled for completion this financial year, subject to the continued availability of funding:Antrim Road, Templepatrick;Largy Road, Crumlin;Church Road, Ballynure;Staffordstown Road, Toome;Ballyutoag Road Footway, Nutts Corner;Portglenone Road, Randalstown; andAhoghill Road, Randalstown.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider the provision of a bus shelter to serve the stop on the Burnside Road at its junction with Moyra Road, Ballyclare.
answer_text: I am pleased to advise that Translink submitted an Economic Appraisal to my Department in June 2022 for the installation of a number of new bus shelters and refurbishment of existing bus shelters across the North including 12 within the South Antrim area, one of which is the shelter at Burnside Road. This Economic Appraisal is being subjected to the necessary approvals process to ensure it represents value for money for the public purse.Subject to approval, the delivery of this project is an operational matter for Translink, and any further queries should, therefore, be directed to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether adults within the Northern Trust are able to be referred for ADHD assessment and support services in other Trusts, given the Northern Trust has not been receiving new referrals since November 2021.
answer_text: NICE guidance (NG 87) recommends that Health and Social Care Trusts should develop multidisciplinary teams with specialist experience in diagnosis and treatment of ADHD in adults. Currently there is no specifically commissioned ADHD Service for Adults. Officials within the Strategic Planning and Performance Group are currently working with regional Mental Health Colleagues to consider service provision and clinical pathways for adults in relation to ADHD. Currently adults within the Northern Trust are not able to be referred for ADHD assessment and support to other Trusts. The NHSCT report commitment to working with commissioners and regional colleagues to develop services for adults requiring ADHD assessment and support. It should be noted individuals have the right of complaint under the formal HSC procedure. Responsibility for dealing with any complaint about Health and Social Care lies with the provider of the service which is the Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health which Health and Social Care Trusts are currently providing ADHD assessment and support services for adults.
answer_text: While ADHD services are not currently commissioned in Northern Ireland, the Belfast, South-Eastern, and Western Trusts have confirmed they do provide support and assessment for those with ADHD. Whilst the Northern Trust provides support they are not accepting new referrals for assessment; instead, it is holding a register of those referred.  Funding for this has been provided internally and the Trusts have stated this is not sustainable in the long term.  The Southern Trust have indicated that they are not adequately resourced to provide support or assessment, in respect of personnel and training.ADHD is a neurodevelopmental condition rather than a mental illness, and expertise in its diagnosis and treatment does not exist naturally within adult mental health services. Licensing of medication for adults is relatively recent, and the majority of knowledge and service structure for ADHD sit within paediatric medicine. You may wish to note that the NICE Clinical Guideline Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder: Diagnosis and Management - NG 87 has been updated (in 2018) and endorsed by the Department of Health (DoH) as applicable to Northern Ireland. The assessment, diagnosis, care and treatment for adults with ADHD will require investment in a commissioned service if the NICE Guideline for Adult ADHD is to be implemented. DoH Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) is currently considering a paper on the position of Adult ADHD services which will be discussed with the Department in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions his Department is taking to improve maternity services for women living in remote and rural areas.
answer_text: Significant progress has been made in addressing the staffing issues faced by the midwifery profession.  A key success has been the increase in the number of pre-registration training places commissioned by my department for midwifery. These places have increased by over 25% to 115 places per annum. Phase 11 of the Delivering Care policy framework has been commissioned by the Chief Nursing Officer to determine a safe and effective staffing model for the Midwifery workforce in Northern Ireland. This work is progressing within the Public Health Agency, and it is hoped there will be a paper proposing the workforce model and requirements for midwifery by the end of 2022.Implementing a Continuity of Midwifery Carer (CoMC) model within Northern Ireland maternity services is crucial in ensuring that all women, babies and families, including those who live in remote and rural areas, have the best and safest midwifery care possible. The specific benefits of the CoMC approach is well documented. Once the wider policy is implemented, it is intended that continuity of midwifery carer will be available for all women, including those women who are pregnant following a previous pregnancy loss or the death of a baby.The Department is planning the transformation of maternity services towards a CoMC model.  This will require investment in staff and resources, so the appointment of a Regional CoMC Midwife Consultant, and a CoMC Implementation Lead Midwife in each Health and Social Care Trust is intended to allow this to be coordinated. This work is pivotal to the new Northern Ireland Maternity Strategy, development of which was paused due to pressures arising from the COVID-19 crisis, but will recommence as soon as possible and accelerate the delivery of a CoMC model here.
