question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline when his Department anticipates that a scheme to resurface the Ballycorr Road, Ballyclare will commence.
answer_text: As you are aware my Department planned to resurface this section of Ballycorr Road in 2019/20.   However, works were put on hold to allow the completion of utility works associated with the new housing development at Craighill Manor.  As a result Ballycorr Road is not currently included within the 2022/23 resurfacing programme.  Once my Department is satisfied that all of the utility works associated with the new development have been completed, the resurfacing work will be re-programmed at the earliest opportunity, subject to the availability of funding.  Therefore, you will hopefully appreciate it is not possible at this stage to confirm when these works will commence.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide further detail on the Stroke Nursing Framework that is referenced in the Stroke Action Plan.
answer_text: I recognise the vital role of the stroke workforce in delivering improved outcomes for those affected by stroke, which is why my Department’s Stroke Action Plan outlines a specific requirement for a Stroke Workforce Review as a priority. The Stroke Nursing Career Framework for Registered Nursing roles and Nursing Assistants will develop job descriptions and core competencies for each of the nursing roles along with the identification of education and learning requirements. In so doing, the Framework will support the outworkings of the Stroke Workforce Review and Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what engagement with stroke survivors and carers has taken place to date in relation to the Stroke Action Plan.
answer_text: My Department’s Reshaping Stroke Care consultation received in excess of 19,000 responses and over 1,600 people attended the seven public consultation events held. The voice of service users and carers have been central in developing the way forward for stroke reform in NI as set out in the recently published Stroke Action Plan. My Department is committed to building on that successful approach. In the context of the wide programme of reform set out in the Action Plan, I have asked my Officials to look for opportunities for engagement as actions are progressed to ensure that the voice of service users, carers and wider stakeholders continues to inform the implementation of reform on the ground.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for (i) an update on the rollout of Project Stratum in the South Antrim Constituency; and (ii) a list of areas within the constituency where work has commenced.
answer_text: As you are aware, Project Stratum was developed to improve connectivity for premises unable to access broadband services of 30 Mbps or greater, primarily across rural areas of Northern Ireland. The project has been allocated £197m of public funding, £150m of which was secured under the Confidence and Supply Agreement between the DUP and the Conservatives. Fibrus Networks was appointed as the contractor in November 2020 and is currently delivering gigabit-capable broadband to 85,000 premises in rural areas across Northern Ireland.The number of premises in the South Antrim Constituency to benefit from Project Stratum is 5,706. Deployment in South Antrim commenced in summer 2022 and will continue until spring 2025. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what work is being carried out by his Department to promote employment opportunities and accessibility for the Deaf community in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) has lead responsibility for employment policy and associated programme delivery, including for those from the Deaf community or those who have disabilities. In terms of skills, my Department provides a wide range of support for people, including those who are Deaf, to access our provision across the further and higher education sectors, in our training, reskilling and upskilling programmes, as well as through the Careers Service.  	Examples of the type of support currently provided include: the Disabled Students Allowance (DSA) for Higher Education students; a focus on disability through the Widening Access and Participation Plans; support through the Additional Support Fund (ASF) in Further Education; extended eligibility, support, and reasonable adjustments on training programmes delivered on behalf of the Department, such as Skills for Life and Work; additional funding through a Disability Supplement to assist participants on apprenticeship programmes (which may include, for example, the provision of resources or sign language-interpreters); and support on programmes such as Skill Up, Skills Focus, Assured Skills and Innovate Us.  		We also recognised through the recently published Skills Strategy for a 10X Economy that more needs to be done to further enhance inclusion. A Skills Equality, Diversity and Inclusion (SEDI) sub-committee of the Northern Ireland Skills Council has been established to look at access and participation for those in under-represented or disadvantaged groups, with the aim of identifying barriers and advising on policy solutions and actions to help drive changes to remove these obstacles.  In addition, my officials are working closely with the Department for Communities to ensure that skills contributions are reflected in their work to develop a Disability Employment Strategy for Northern Ireland. 		I trust this answer is helpful in relation to my Department’s commitment in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on a Sign Language GCSE being made available.
answer_text: The Department for Education in England is working closely with subject experts, stakeholders and Ofqual, the independent qualifications regulator (England), to develop the draft subject content for a British Sign Language (BSL) GCSE. They will be consulting on this draft and then, once it is agreed, interested awarding bodies will be required to develop specifications. Once this work is completed and a qualification becomes available it can then be accredited for use in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the progress of sign language legislation in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials are finalising work on proposals for a Sign Language Bill. However, given the cross cutting nature of such proposals, legislation cannot proceed without Executive approval.I remain committed to bringing forward Sign Language legislation built on the principles of equality and social inclusion in ensuring that the Deaf community here have the same rights and opportunities as those in the hearing community and are able to access services in their own language.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether work is underway in his Department to promote accessibility on the public transport network for Deaf people.
answer_text: The accessibility and inclusivity of our public transport network is vitally important for me and I am fully committed to providing inclusive and accessible Public Transport and facilities for all customers. I am pleased to advise that both my Department and Translink work closely with the Inclusive Mobility and Transport Advisory Committee (IMTAC) and value the contributions and advice of public transport users with lived experience of disabilities to inform how we can continually make improvements to the accessibility of the network.  Translink’s website has been independently accredited by The Shaw Trust charity achieving 100% compliance with (WCAG 2.1 AA) web accessibility standards. For any passengers who are deaf and require support from Tranlink’s Contact Centre, an online interpreter service is provided via their website, offering both British and Irish Sign Language services via a three-way video conversation between the Contact Centre Agent, the interpreter and customer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration her Department has given to the teaching of Sign Language in primary and secondary schools.
answer_text: As you are aware, the Department for Communities is working towards the commitment in the Executive’s New Decade, New Approach agreement to introduce a Sign Language Bill.  My officials will want to consider the impact and operational practicalities of any proposals including the impact on the curriculum.In terms of what is currently taught in our schools, the Northern Ireland curriculum is broad and balanced, providing schools with considerable flexibility to adapt what they teach and to use approaches that best suit the learning needs of their pupils.  This flexibility affords the freedom to include learning opportunities, such as sign language, should a school consider this a priority for their pupils.Furthermore, ‘Communication’ is one of the three Cross-Curricular Skills at the heart of our curriculum.  Pupils are given opportunities to engage with and demonstrate the skill of communication and to transfer their knowledge about communication concepts and skills to real-life meaningful contexts across the curriculum.  Modes of effective communication include talking and listening, reading and writing, but also non-verbal modes of communication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department will publish the budgetary information relating to Health Service transformation strategies.
answer_text: As the Department of Health (DoH) has primary policy responsibility for Health transformation strategies, that department is best placed to provide the budgetary information you are seeking. As part of the draft Budget exercise, DoH identified a number of pressures in relation to Health transformation. The draft Budget proposed that the department’s bids for the Elective Care, Cancer and Mental Health strategies were funded in full, providing £120.9m / £182.4m / £255.3m over the three year budget period from 2022-23 to 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the reasons for the increase in those leaving the medical and dental professions, as noted in the recently published March 2022 Health and Social Care Workforce Census.
answer_text: HSC leavers and joiners in the March 2022 publication were calculated by comparing the snapshots of individuals employed at 31 March 2021 with individuals employed at 31 March 2022.  This included permanent and temporary contracts only.  Those who appeared on the March 2022 dataset, but not March 2021, were classed as joiners.  Those who appeared on the March 2021 dataset, but not March 2022, were classed as leavers.  This means that the analysis relates to leavers from, and joiners to, HSC permanent/temporary employment in the previous year.  However, it is important to note that they may not have left the medical or dental professions.  Doctors in training are excluded from the analysis due to the planned rotation of posts during their training.  This means that HSC joiner figures will not include the newly qualified; nor will it count as joiners the HSC doctors in training in March 2021 who by March 2022 have achieved an HSC consultant or another post e.g. specialty doctor. The overall position shows that there was a net increase in the total of HSC Medical & Dental staff in post (excluding doctors in training) in March 2022 compared to the previous year (by a count of 72 staff in post or 69.5 whole-time equivalent), see table below. Much of this net increase resulted from doctors in training achieving the Certificate of Completion of Training (CCT) and gaining HSC consultant posts by March 2022.Medical & Dental (excluding doctors in training categories)Count of staff in postWhole-time equivalentAt 31 March 20212,9972,634.5At 31 March 20223,0692,704.0Net Change7269.5Source: HSC Workforce Census March 2021 and HSC Workforce Census March 2022In addition, it is likely that the last couple of years of joiners and leavers trends have been disrupted by temporary Workforce Appeal appointments which last between 3 and 6 months.I remain fully committed to expanding our local workforce to meet identified needs and rebuild of our health and social care system.  I announced a £2.1m healthcare education and training package on 11 May 2022, including an additional 13 medical specialty training places.  The Northern Ireland Medical Foundation Programme also received 19 additional places above the current 252 baseline.I added 10 GP training places in 2022/23, building on continuing work to invest in our GP workforce which has seen the number of GP trainees increase by over 70% from 2015/16 levels.  Finally the number of filled Foundation Dentist posts in 2022/23 increased by 3 (10%) over the previous year, providing some additional capacity. However, much more needs to be done.  Accordingly, on 15 June 2022 I published the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy Second Action Plan (2022-23 to 2024-25), setting out a challenging range of measures to train, recruit and retain healthcare staff to ensure that we have a workforce of the optimum size and with the right range of skills. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the cross-departmental work that has been undertaken on budgetary estimates for the Department of Health’s Second Action Plan.
answer_text: The Department of Health (DoH) has primary policy responsibility for the HSC Workforce Strategy and the associated Second Action Plan, and therefore DoH has primary responsibility for identifying the budgetary implications of the Action Plan.  As part of the draft Budget 2022-25 exercise, departments were asked to identify their pressures to enable the Executive to consider the allocation of funding across 2022/23 – 2024/25 Budget period.You will be aware that in the absence of an Executive, it is not possible to set a Budget; so my department has worked with departments to put in place Contingency Planning Envelopes in order to assist them in making decisions on the allocation of resources in 2022/23.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given soaring dental inflation, the impact of COVID and a 40 per cent reduction in dental earnings, whether a cost of service investigation by a third party provider is required for health service dentistry.
answer_text: The current focus of my Department is to rebuild General Dental Services so that the immediate oral health needs of the public are met. In parallel the Dental Contract Reform Group is exploring options to ensure that Health Service dentistry is sustainable and delivers for both patients and the profession. Undertaking a Cost of Service Review will be an important part of any new approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when will the Welfare Mitigation Review be published.
answer_text: The Welfare Mitigations independent advisory panel have presented their final report, including recommendations for new mitigation measures, to the Department. The report is under consideration and will be published at the earliest opportunity. The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how he plans to rebuild confidence among General Dental Practitioners in NHS dentistry; and (ii) whether maintaining the 25 per cent Rebuilding Support Scheme enhancement to fees is considered a key way to achieve this.
answer_text: My Department continues to engage with the dental industry via the British Dental Association (BDA). In 2021 the Contract Reform Group was established to work with representatives from the BDA in a collaborative approach, to examine how to make improvements in the dental contract in the short and longer term to ensure that Health Service dentistry is sustainable and delivers for both patients and the profession.As was communicated to General Dental Practitioners by a letter issued on the 21st of September 2022, the Rebuilding Support Scheme will be extended until the end of 2022/23 with a 10% enhancement on Item of Service payments. This is further investment, projected to be a further £2m, in a key service that has received significant support since the outset of the pandemic.  The scheme was established specifically in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. With the impact of the pandemic significantly reducing over the past number of months and most restrictions now removed, the justification for ongoing COVID-specific support has also reduced.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on when the second installment of the cost of living payments will be issued.
answer_text: rThe second Low Income Cost of Living Payment qualifying date will be no later than 31 October 2022.The link below for NI Direct will be updated with the dates for the second Low Income Cost of Living Payments when they are announced.https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/cost-living-support

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the cost of living crisis, will there be any financial support packages for pensioners.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to address (i) the pay disparity affecting hospital dentists in Northern Ireland, including trainee pay; (ii) the absence of new Clinical Excellence Awards; and (iii) pensions mitigations.
answer_text: Medical and Dental pay issues are a devolved matter in Northern Ireland. Medical and Dental trainees in England have been on a different contract to those in Northern Ireland since 2016. Any change to the NI contract would be a matter for negotiation between the Department and the BMA. Following mediation in August 2022, the Department is now working collaboratively with the BMA to Co-design a new future Clinical Excellence Scheme.  Work is currently underway to introduce guidance for pension flexibilities, including contribution recycling, which may help those who are negatively impacted by pension tax charges. It is hoped employers will be in a position to offer this from next year. There are a number of policy issues being considered at UK level looking at the impact of inflation rates and possible flexible retirement measures. Departmental officials are in regular contact with GB counterparts to ensure they are kept up to date on any possible changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether, following a recent BBC investigation that found 90% of dental practices are not taking on new NHS adult patients and 88% are unable to take on new child patients, additional investment is needed in dentistry.
answer_text: The Department responded to the pressures facing dentistry, caused by the pandemic, by investing more: £76m in support payments through 2020/21 and 2021/22.In the first half of 2022/23 the Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS) has invested an additional £4.1 in dental practices by paying dentists an additional 25% for dental treatments.  The RSS has been extended until the end of 2022/23, enhancing payments to dentists by 10%, expected to cost an additional £2m.Additional investment will also be made in the form of the Urgent Care for Unregistered Patients Scheme and work is ongoing in relation to further investment in the registration and treatment of children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the future for NHS dentistry, in particular finding the additional investment required to deliver a new General Dental Services contract at a time of constrained public finances and urgent pressures, including increasing expenses for dental practitioners.
answer_text: Prior to the arrival of COVID-19, General Dental Services (GDS) in Northern Ireland faced many challenges, however access to treatment was good and the general oral health of the population was improving.  The impact of the Covid-19 pandemic on delivery of dental services was significant, indeed the effects are still being felt, even as restrictions have been lifted.  To help sustain GDS during the Covid-19 pandemic, my Department provided £76m in financial support to independent General Dental Practitioners over the last 2 years. Further funding was committed for the first half of 2022/23 to support dental practices, with dental fees being enhanced by 25% as part of the Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS), to reflect the ongoing pandemic pressures practices are facing. This support has been extended at 10% until the end of this financial year. This investment has been supplemented by an additional £6.5m made available to practices via Revenue Grant Schemes in 2020/21 and 2021/22.Additional investment will also be made in the form of the Urgent Care for Unregistered Patients Scheme and work is ongoing in relation to further investment in the registration and treatment of children.However investment alone will not deliver the improvements we need and that is why the Department’s Dental Contract Reform Group (CRG) was established.  The CRG is looking at how to make improvement in the dental contract in short and longer term to ensure that Health Service dentistry is sustainable and delivers for both patients and the profession.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will raise the Carer’s Allowance earnings threshold to the equivalent of 16 hours a week at the Real Living Wage.
answer_text: I appointed an independent panel to complete a comprehensive review of existing welfare mitigation measures and to consider future measures. The panel has provided its final report to my Department and this will be published at the earliest opportunity.		The implementation of any additional payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department intends to integrate the recommendations of the policy solutions white paper published by Carers NI with existing strategies on health service transformation, specifically regarding unpaid carers.
answer_text: Whilst the cost of living crisis is affecting all areas of society, I recognise that informal/family carers can face particular challenges, which can exacerbate the impact of increases in the cost of living.In regards to the recommendations made by Carers NI in their recent white paper, and how these would integrate with existing strategies on health service transformation. I note that a substantial number of the recommendations, particularly those relating to the impact of the cost of living, would fall under the remit of other government departments, rather than directly for the Department of Health. It would be for those departments to consider as appropriate. I recognise however that there are many improvements to be made, and more action needs to be taken to support carers.My Department has recently undertaken a public consultation on proposals to Reform the Adult Social Care system in Northern Ireland. A central priority of the plans is supporting family and unpaid carers and this consultation included a number of proposals to improve the support available.The consultation included a proposal to build on the work of existing HSCT carers’ registers to develop and maintain a voluntary central register of carers, with the aim of improved identification and data availability of carers, especially those who may need support.The development of a more robust central list of carers could facilitate improved provision of support to carers, such as that suggested in the Carers NI paper. We should not however underestimate the challenges of developing, and maintaining, such a comprehensive register and it is likely to be a long term piece of work that would require sustained attention and additional resources.Now that the consultation period has ended, my officials are analysing the responses and a summary and evaluation report will be prepared for Ministerial consideration. The Department will publish the consultation report, which will then inform the development of a new strategy for adult social care. I look forward to seeing the outcome of the consultation and views in relation to the proposals to further support carers.I remain committed to supporting carers, and to seeking to find additional ways to provide support where we can, and where pressures allow. In this respect, I was pleased to be able to launch the Support for Carers’ Fund at the end of March 2021. This Fund, worth over £4m, is providing much needed financial resources to the community and voluntary sector to support carers. I am pleased to advise that significant funding of approximately £2.5m has already been awarded to projects which are supporting real improvements in the lives and experiences of individual carers There has never before been funding of this scale available to support carers in Northern Ireland and I hope this is an indication of the value I place on the role they play.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department will ensure that medicine remains an attractive career option.
answer_text: My Department has established the Strategic Group to Enhance the Quality of Medical Education in Northern Ireland (SGEQMENI) to take forward practical measures to make Northern Ireland an attractive place to learn and practise medicine.  This Group includes representatives from the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency, Queen’s University Belfast, Ulster University and the HSC Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will take action to incentivise GP's to remain in work by providing the indemnity cover that their counterparts in the rest of the UK have.
answer_text: Work has recommenced to review arrangements for the provision of indemnity cover for GPs in Northern Ireland.  A range of options are under consideration from the status quo to alternatives such as the state backed systems adopted in England and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what Departmental strategies are in place to ensure doctors do not leave the profession, reduce hours or retire earlier than planned so that expertise and capacity can be retained in the system.
answer_text: My Department published the comprehensive Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for Our People in May 2018.  The aim of the Strategy is that ‘by 2026, we meet our workforce needs and the needs of our workforce’.My Department published the Strategy’s second action plan on 15th June 2022 to cover the period 2022/23 to 2024/25.  The second action plan contains a total of 32 actions and 94 associated outputs that were developed collaboratively with stakeholders during 2021/22 and provide the strategic direction for the next three years to meet our workforce needs and the needs of our workforce.  While retention of staff is an intended outcome of the actions identified across the Strategy, the second action plan contains a specific action to develop and deliver retention initiatives that are tailored to the requirements of individual healthcare professions including doctors.One issue which can impact doctors later in their career is pension tax charges and work is underway to introduce guidance for flexibilities which may help those who are negatively impacted. There are currently a number of policy issues being considered at UK level looking at the impact of inflation rates and possible flexible retirement measures. Departmental officials are in regular contact with GB counterparts to ensure they are kept up to date on any possible changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will award a pay uplift for doctors commensurate with the current rate of inflation.
answer_text: I recently publicly acknowledged the need to ensure all staff are appropriately paid, when highlighting the financial pressures and budgetary uncertainty facing our health service. I must stress that our pay award decisions can also only be taken in the context of a wider public sector pay policy set by the Department of Finance. Any pay increase will also require to be fully funded by the Executive, given the financial outlook facing my Department in 2022/23.I further issued a statement on 20th July, in which I set out that I have considered the recommendations made by the DDRB, and would be content to recommend acceptance of the relevant award.However, unlike my colleagues in other parts of the United Kingdom, I am unable to announce the immediate implementation of these pay awards locally as Northern Ireland still does not have an agreed Executive Budget for 2022/23. Equally, I welcome and support the recent letter from the three devolved Finance Ministers asking the Chancellor to increase budgets to address pay and other pressures, including in the Health Service.The current cost of living crisis impacts on us all, yet Departments cannot address all issues in isolation – we need a functioning Executive to deliver appropriate support across our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will commit to securing the necessary funds to roll-out services under the 3-year Community Pharmacy Commissioning Plan.
answer_text: Implementation of the Community Pharmacy Commissioning Plan 22-25, for year one (2022-23) is fully funded. In the absence of an agreed budget for 2022-23 I am having to carefully manage within the resources available to my Department and this has limited my ability to commit additional funding to services.  The Finance Minister wrote to Departments in March, which involved the Department of Finance issuing Departments with contingency planning envelopes for the 2022-23 financial year.  These envelopes provided Departments with an assessment of the minimum funding they could reasonably expect for 2022-23 and allowed them to plan for expenditure until such times as a Budget could be agreed. I have agreed an approach for my Department to proceed with opening allocations which will continue to fund activity in the first quarter of 2022/23 at previous levels while controlling spending in line with the advice from the Finance Minister. I am very conscious of pressing demands for funding across many areas of health and social care, and as Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.  		The structures relating to implementation of HSC transformation are currently under review. Details of this will be communicated shortly, once decisions are taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the publication of the new Reshaping Stroke Care Action Plan, what plans his Department has to prevent more strokes through better treatment of risk factors.
answer_text: My Department recognises that stroke is preventable. The Reshaping Stroke Care Action Plan sets out steps taken to date to improve stroke prevention including the endorsement of a range of guidance from the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence.Going forward, the Action Plan sets out a commitment to ensure seven day access to Transient Ischaemic Attack (TIA) assessment within 24 hours of stroke symptoms. More widely, my Department will continue to be guided by the evidence base regarding population level interventions such as screening and will consider additional actions in that context.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline (i) what steps he has taken to urgently address the backlog of MOT appointments due; and (ii) how his Department is ensuring motorists are informed about the latest MOT and vehicle tax advice.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  The DVA is monitoring the number of applications received and tests carried out and expects waiting times to reduce over time through the actions they have taken to increase vehicle testing capacity across the network. The DVA continues to experience significant demand for vehicle testing services and in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires.  In such cases, customers must book the earliest available test appointment for their vehicle, which may mean travelling to another test centre instead of their preferred location.  The DVA releases short notice vehicle test appointments on a daily basis to ensure testing can be delivered when staffing availability is confirmed.  Customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available. The DVA’s advice to vehicle owners who require an MOT to tax their vehicle and cannot secure a date before their MOT expires is to:book the earliest available appointment; andkeep checking the booking system for an earlier appointment.If they get to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an MOT appointment or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment slot.The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/book-view-change-or-cancel-mot-vehicle-test  and is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to their customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department has taken to ensure those diagnosed with metastatic breast cancer have access to specialist nurses with expertise in secondary breast cancer.
answer_text: Specialist nurses have a key role in ensuring that all people with cancer, including those with metastatic breast cancer, have their holistic needs assessed, the development of personalised care plans and facilitating self-management and receive appropriate longer term follow up.Action 39 of the Cancer Strategy, which I launched in March 2022, is to provide all patients, including children and young people, diagnosed with cancer access to a Clinical Nurse Specialist throughout the entire care pathway. Implementation of this action will further enhance the access to specialist nurses with expertise in secondary breast cancer.My Department continues to invest considerable time and money in ensuring there is access to specialist nurses across cancer services.   However I am remain conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care.  As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.  My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty. Funding elements of the health service on a month to month or quarter to quarter basis is not an efficient or effective way to build for the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department has taken to ensure that people in Northern Ireland, diagnosed with metastatic breast cancer, have access to the same range of pharmaceutical and other treatments available in other regions of the UK.
answer_text: The Cancer Strategy, launched in March 2022, has a strong focus on clinical trials and research with its actions committing to addressing issues for all people with cancer in Northern Ireland, including those with secondary or metastatic disease. The need to improve access to trials for metastatic breast is recognised and is a strategic priority for the National Cancer Research Institute Breast group and our local trials unit is linked into this work.The Northern Ireland Implementation Plan for Clinical Research Recovery, Resilience and Growth (https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-implementation-plan-annex-a.pdf) is bringing together key stakeholders across clinical research in order to provide a strengthened framework and approach to research locally and nationally. Access to treatment within Northern Ireland generally follows that of NHS England and NICE, ensuring that people here diagnosed with metastatic breast cancer, have access to the same range of pharmaceutical and other treatments available in other regions of the UK. NICE approved systemic anticancer therapies are made available in Northern Ireland with immediate effect. Treatments are made available in line with their approved indication and are subject to a clinical assessment that confirms the patient meets the indication for which the drug is approved. In addition, where off-license drug alternatives exist and are deemed clinically appropriate, clinicians can apply for them via the Individual Funding Request process.  Where there are other evidence-based diagnostics or treatments routinely commissioned by NHS England that are not available within NI (e.g. certain types of specialist surgery or radiotherapy) these can be accessed via the Extra Contractual Referral (ECR) process.  Alternative treatments that are not evidence-based or NICE approved are not funded or commissioned in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the measures taken by his Department to alert public bodies to the new autism legislation and associated compliance measures; and (ii) how these compare with the awareness measures taken following the enactment of the Autism Act (NI) 2011.
answer_text: Departments, Health and Social Care Trusts and regional multi-agency autism stakeholder forums, comprised of public bodies, were advised of the progress and implications of the legislation throughout the legislative passage.  Upon receiving Royal Assent a letter issued to all Departments outlining the clauses being introduced and advising them of their responsibility in the implementation of the Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022.  A request was also made, in this correspondence, for a member of staff to be nominated as a designated point of contact for autism.  A first meeting of these nominees took place on 26th May 2022.You will appreciate that there have been numerous staffing changes in my Department since the Autism Act (NI) 2011 was enacted and it would take significant resource to investigate Departmental records to compare the awareness raising measures between the two legislative instruments; therefore I am unable to advise you of the measures which were taken at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education further to the Assembly motion on 3 February 2020 and the additional training duties introduced by the Autism (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, what consideration her Department has given to introducing mandatory autism training for teaching staff.
answer_text: In response to the motion in February 2020, the Department commissioned Middletown Centre for Autism (MCA) and the Education Authority (EA) to consider existing autism training for the educational workforce and identify any gaps.Subsequently the Enhanced Autism Training Framework (EATF) was launched which provides a tiered approach training offer (from basic understanding/awareness raising courses to a Post-Graduate Certificate in Autism Studies) which are available to all teachers, class-room assistants and other educational professionals via the MCA and EA training teams.    In light of the Autism (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, the Department is currently reviewing the EATF, in conjunction with MCA and EA, to ensure that the training offered is in line with the Department’s new duties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the (i) frequency; (ii) outcome; and (iii) planning work of inter-departmental discussions and correspondence regarding the implementation of the Autism (Amendment) Act 2022.
answer_text: Following correspondence to the Permanent Secretary of each Department, nominations were received to establish a Cross-Departmental Autism Working Group which convened for the first meeting on 26 May 2022.  The Group will meet on a quarterly basis.At this meeting the Group were made aware of the implications of the Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022 and the responsibility of departments and the Group in its implementation.  Following this meeting a monitoring framework on the implementation of the current Autism - Interim Strategy 2021-2022 issued to all departments. Members will also participate in workshops being held in September to determine measures and outcomes for the Autism Strategy 2023-2028.Members of the Group will provide a designated point of contact in each Department for the independent Autism Reviewer upon appointmentA response in relation to correspondence regarding the implementation of the legislation has been provided to you in AQW 2445/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions he has taken to progress the resourcing and appointment process of the independent office of the Autism Reviewer.
answer_text: Appointment to this role will be undertaken through the public appointments process.  Officials are currently engaging with the office of the Commissioner for Public Appointments to commence development and the appointment process for the role.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the £150 disability payment component will be paid, as part of the package of direct cost-of-living payments.
answer_text: The disability additional payment of £150 will be paid to all eligible claimants from September 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what engagement her Department has had to ensure that PSNI recruitment is progressed for those who have successfully completed all assessments prior to obtaining a place at the Police Training College.
answer_text: I meet regularly with the Chief Constable and his senior team to discuss a range of issues.  However, as the terms of Section 33 of The Police (NI) Act 2000 state the PSNI is under the direction and control of the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board, recruitment remains a matter for the Chief Constable.  I remain committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the roll-out of the £500 special recognition payment for Independent Care Home employees who worked during the qualifying period but have since left their employer.
answer_text: Phase 2 had been temporarily paused to allow time to seek clarity on a number of issues raised relating to the impact of the increase in national insurance contributions which are included as part of the payment provided to the former employer.  A further letter was issued to employers on 14 April 2022 providing clarity on the issues raised and confirming recommencement of the payment process. Claims are being processed by BSO in date order when all relevant information is provided by the claimants. No date has been set for the closure of the scheme and claims will continue to be processed by the Business Services Organisation during 2022/23. I can confirm that as at 16th June 2022, a total of over £16.67m has been paid out under Phase 1 to independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers covering 28,470 individuals. As at the same date over £4.22m has been paid out to 9,857 individuals under Phase 2; these payments include staff previously employed in independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers who have since moved employer or left the sector, and staff employed with eligible day opportunity providers and buildings-based day services.In addition we have also opened the Scheme to eligible applicants in the following groups, Personal Assistants employed by Independent Living Fund recipients, Thalidomide Health Grant recipients, and Personal Assistants employed by those in receipt of Direct Payments from HSC Trusts. This element of the scheme was launched on 23 March and is being delivered by ILF Scotland for the payments, Scotland Excel who set up and run the online portal, and SDS Scotland who manage the dedicated helpline. These delivery partners were heavily involved in delivering the equivalent scheme for the Scottish Government.  To date £419k has been paid. To encourage uptake on this element Trusts have now written to all individuals in receipt of direct payments. I can confirm that on the 17th June 2022 my officials launched an online portal which will allow those in the independent sector whose employers have ceased trading to apply for the Special Recognition Payment.Staff who worked in the independent sector (care home/domiciliary care/supported living) whose employer is no longer trading are eligible to apply for this payment if they were employed for a continuous 4 week period between 17 March 2020 and 31 January 2021. Applications can be made online through the following link: https://www.smartsurvey.co.uk/s/SRPnolongertrading/ A dedicated helpline is also available from 10am – 12.30pm Monday to Friday. The helpline number is: 0800 170 13 50. In addition, a full list of Frequently Asked Questions is available through the link below online for those in the independent sector whose employers have ceased trading: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/special-recognition-payments-former-employees-where-provider-has-ceased-trading A FAQ on the Special Recognition Payment has been published on the Department’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department has taken following public concerns about the services provided by Logan Wellbeing Clinic.
answer_text: My Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group is investigating the claims regarding the alleged unlawful and inappropriate advertising and use of medicines within Logan Wellbeing and Medical. My officials are also assisting the Police Service of Northern Ireland with inquiries.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy why all properties within BT39 9PB are not included within the coverage of Project Stratum.
answer_text: Project Stratum was developed to improve connectivity for premises unable to access broadband services of 30 Megabits per second or greater, primarily across rural areas of Northern Ireland. The contract to make services available to the defined intervention area was awarded to Fibrus Networks in November 2020.There are 22 premises in the postcode area of BT39 9PB, with one premises included in Project Stratum. The remaining premises are not eligible for Project Stratum for one of the following reasons:•	According to the information available from Pointer (the address database for Northern Ireland, maintained by Land & Property Services) at the time that the intervention area for Project Stratum was being developed, the premises was not built and approved as of January 2019.•	Another broadband provider submitted information to my Department which indicated that a broadband service of at least 30 Mbps was available at the premises, or would be available by January 2022.The eligibility status of each individual premises can be checked at: www.hyperfastni.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when residential respite provision facilities for children with autism or behavioural problems will return to pre-pandemic levels.
answer_text: Respite services play a critical role in supporting disabled children, young people and their families to stay together and reduce the need for intensive late interventions by Health & Social Care.  Due to increasing complexity presented by children with disabilities, there has been growing demand for short breaks and long term residential care placements, which has been further impacted by the Covid-19 pandemic. Respite services for families with children with complex needs including children with Autism, Learning Disabilities or both continue to be prioritised on the assessed needs of the individual and their family.  It is however not possible to isolate the services provided specifically for families with children with autism or behavioural difficulties.  There is a recognition at a regional and departmental level that there are insufficient short breaks and residential care placements to meet the growing complexity of Children with Disabilities (CwD).  To meet the future service needs, the HSCB (now the Department’s Strategic Planning & Performance Group) developed a Regional Framework for Children’s Disability Services, which proposes a new overarching model of care for children with a disability in Northern Ireland.  This draft framework outlines a new model of practice based on the principles of: (i) early intervention; (ii) collaboration and multi-agency working; (iii) co-design and decision making with families; and (iv) person centred care.The implementation of the framework will be underpinned by four strategic work streams, which will require significant transformation across Health & Social Care services:improving our early offer of help and community based supports; developing additional effective residential short breaks, fostering and intensive family support;rethinking our approach to residential provision and how we support CwD effectively in out of home placements; andgetting Transition right The CwD draft framework is currently under consideration by the Department.  It should however be noted that the recommendations set out in the framework will require significant re-design of existing services, workforce development and investment.In relation to the restoration of residential respite provision facilities for children with autism or behavioural problems will return to pre-pandemic levels, in February 2022, the Chief Social Work Officer wrote to Trusts to outline my decision to remobilise respite services.  To date, significant efforts have been made by Trusts to implement the Public Health Agency Pathway to manage risk, while increasing service provision on a phased basis, throughout a period of high community prevalence of Covid-19. The latest position reported by Trusts shows that the majority have increased availability in adult day care. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans there are to increase provision for respite care for families with children with autism or behavioural difficulties.
answer_text: Respite services play a critical role in supporting disabled children, young people and their families to stay together and reduce the need for intensive late interventions by Health & Social Care.  Due to increasing complexity presented by children with disabilities, there has been growing demand for short breaks and long term residential care placements, which has been further impacted by the Covid-19 pandemic. Respite services for families with children with complex needs including children with Autism, Learning Disabilities or both continue to be prioritised on the assessed needs of the individual and their family.  It is however not possible to isolate the services provided specifically for families with children with autism or behavioural difficulties. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many places are available for residential respite care for families with children or young people who have autism or behavioural difficulties.
answer_text: Residential respite care is managed by each individual Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust; as my Department does not routinely collect data in regards to this question, information has been sourced from the HSC Trusts.The Belfast HSC Trust does not deliver residential respite services specifically for children and young people with autism or behavioural difficulties. However, the Trust does have access to six respite beds across two residential homes for children who have moderate to severe learning disabilities and associated challenging behaviours; many of these children also have autism.The Northern HSC Trust has a statutory respite unit with six beds, but the capacity and allocation of these varies dependent on the needs of the children. Additionally, they have access, via a contract, to an unspecified number of beds provided by short break and foster carers.  The South Eastern HSC Trust does not currently provide respite to children with a diagnosis of autism only. A need for this has been identified, and once resources are available they intend to provide this service in Newcastle alongside the services available to children and young people with learning and physical disabilities. In the Southern HSC Trust, the Children with Disabilities Service provides Residential Short Break beds within two facilities for children and young people with a dual diagnosis of Autism and Behavioural challenges. The service currently has five to eight Residential Short Break placements available per night. The service does not provide overnight residential respite provision for children and young people that have a single diagnosis of autism; however, dependent on the assessment, their needs can be met through the Children with Disabilities short breaks fostering service.In the Western HSC Trust, the provision of residential short breaks for all families of children with a disability is based on assessed need rather than diagnosis.  The Trust currently has two beds available for two overnights once a fortnight.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider The Thompson Primary School, Ballyrobert, for inclusion in a future rollout of the part-time 20mph speed limit zones at schools.
answer_text: The second tranche of part-time 20mph speed limits at schools is currently being rolled out and upon completion, over 200 schools across the North will be benefitting from this initiative. When all of the tranche 2 sites are fully operational and have had sufficient time to bed in, my Department intends to carry out a review of this work to determine if a further tranche is justified. Until this review is carried out I am not in a position to commit to install part-time 20mph speed limits at any more schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 31092/17-22, for an update on the process to extend the existing 30mph speed limit on Randalstown Road.
answer_text: I am pleased to be able to confirm that the legislative process to extend the existing 30mph speed limit on Randalstown Road, Antrim is already underway which it is anticipated will be completed later this year.I understand the proposal also has the full support of the PSNI and Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the delivery of the Housing Executive's External Cyclical Maintenance Scheme in Ballyduff, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that an External Cyclical Maintenance scheme for Ballyduff, Newtownabbey is currently programmed for a start on site in August 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider lowering the speed limit to 30mph on the Park Road, from it's junction with Mallusk Road, to the vicinity of the Poplars development.
answer_text: My Department will carry out a review of the existing speed limit on the Park Road, Mallusk, in accordance with our Policy and Procedure Guide (RSPPG) E051 ‘Setting Local Speed Limits in Northern Ireland’, and also consult with the PSNI Roads Policing Unit, and advise you of the outcome in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on proposals to implement a 1.75 per cent pay increase for Housing Executive staff, including any backdated pay that is owed.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to my answer to AQW 398/22-27 on the same matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on progress with the School Enhancement Programme business case for Thompson Primary School, Ballyrobert.
answer_text: As a controlled school, the Education Authority (EA) is undertaking the delivery of the School Enhancement Programme project at the Thompson Primary School. The EA has advised that an Integrated Consultancy Team (ICT) has been appointed to take the scheme from technical feasibility through to completion. The ICT has issued a Technical Feasibility Report to the EA, which looks at several options to achieve the requirements of the brief. These options will be reviewed in the business case. The business case has been initiated and the Non-Monetary Workshop is to be arranged for September 2022, following which the business case will be completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what actions his Department will take to support employees who may be impacted by the closure of nine branches of the Ulster Bank.
answer_text: My Department’s Careers Service works closely with the Department for Communities (DfC) to ensure people who are facing redundancy are fully informed about current job and reskilling opportunities. While no official notice has been received from the Ulster Bank about possible redundancies, my officials will ensure the employer is aware of the help available, with personalised support offered in all nine locations to any employee who wishes to discuss their future career.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on any investigation or testing carried out in conjunction with Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council at Lough Neagh, following the recent death of two pets.
answer_text: I was sorry to hear about the recent deaths of two young dogs in Rea’s Wood area of Lough Neagh. Officials in NIEA continue to liaise and work closely with colleagues in Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council (ANBC) to assist them with their investigation. NIEA officials have inspected the area for visual signs of water pollution. No blue green algae were found in water samples examined at the Water Management Unit in Lisburn.  A water sample was taken for a broad sweep of organic substances and nothing of significance was detected. The substances that were identified are commonly found at low levels in rivers and lakes across NI. The results of all our analyses have been shared with ANBC to assist with their investigation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 23116/17-22, for an update on the development of future legislation for dealing with dilapidated and derelict properties.
answer_text: The Dilapidation Bill Team has been re-established now that it has finished work on producing the Environment Strategy for Northern Ireland.Officials are currently in the process of finalising the substantial economic appraisal which must be completed before the Bill can be introduced.It is my hope that the Bill can be introduced as soon as practicable in this mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the scheduled roll-out of Project Stratum within South Antrim.
answer_text: Project Stratum was allocated £165m of public funding, £150m of which was secured under the Confidence and Supply Agreement between the Democratic Unionist Party and the Conservatives, and £15m from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs. In November 2020 Fibrus Networks was appointed as the contractor to deliver gigabit-capable broadband to over 76,000 premises, using the £165m of public funding, and their own substantial investment of more than £45m. In January 2022, my Department secured the additional capital funding of £32m to bring a further 8,469 premises into Project Stratum, including 503 premises in the South Antrim Constituency. The inclusion of these additional premises will bring the total number of premises in the South Antrim Constituency to benefit from Project Stratum to 5,706.Current deployment plans show that the first premises in the South Antrim Constituency are scheduled for improvement in summer 2022, with deployment continuing until spring 2025. The inclusion of the additional premises will not change the deployment schedule for those premises originally included in Project Stratum.The table below provides a breakdown of the deployment schedule within the South Antrim Constituency by number of premises. Further information for individual premises can be found on the website: www.hyperfastni.com. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Hillhead Road, Ballyclare, will be scheduled for resurfacing as part of the Department’s 2022/23 resurfacing programme.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department still envisages that resurfacing of Hillhead Road will take place in the summer of 2023, subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31184/17-22, at what stage is the business case for 48 additional beds at Antrim Area Hospital.
answer_text: A new Outline Business Case for 48 additional beds at Antrim Area Hospital was received by my Department on 5th May 2022.It is currently being reviewed by Departmental advisors.
